title: Learn to Stop: Understanding Constraints Helps Designers and Businesses Thrive
summary: Designers often aim for perfection. When we create a feature, we want to refine it over and over until it feels just right. This attention to detail is part of what makes good designers great. But many of us struggle with knowing when to stop. Design is an iterative process, not a one-time exercise. Teams that wait until a design is “perfect” before shipping often miss the mark—because perfection is subjective, timelines are real, and businesses need to move forward. Instead, we should focus on building consistently, delivering value with each iteration, and clearly defining what’s in and out of scope.

approaches to testing asynchronous code in ava cript: - sing promises - sing async/await - sing callbacks n this article, will illustrate each approach using examples from the est- riven evelopment for ava cript https://learning.oreilly.com/course/test-driven-development-for/9780137864591/ course. he production code we will test is a simple do ask function that simulates a task with random success or failure after a delay: javascript function do ask task umber, random umber { return new romise function resolve, reject { set imeout = { if random umber < 0.5 resolve ask ${task umber} resolved else reject ask ${task umber} rejected }, random umber 5000 } } sing romises ccording to https://developer.mozilla.org/en- /docs/ eb/ ava cript/ eference/ lobal bjects/ romise , he romise resolves if value < 0.5", = { return do ask 1, 0.3 .then data = { expect data .to e " ask 1 resolved" } } hen testing rejection, it’s essential to add expect.assertions n to ensure the test verifies 1, 0.5 .catch err = { expect err .to e " ask 1 rejected" } } est provides resolves and rejects matchers for simpler syntax when working with promises, so we can use these too: javascript test "task rejected" } sing sync/ wait he async/await syntax simplifies working with promises, allowing us to write asynchronous code in a synchronous style. ith async/await , we use await to pause execution until the promise resolves, and then we run our matcher: javascript test "task resolves if value < 0.5", async = { const data = await do ask 1, 0.3 expect data .to e " ask 1 resolved" } hen testing expect.assertions 1 try { await do ask 1, 0.5 } catch err { expect err .to e " ask 1 rejected" } } sing callbacks esting with callbacks is less common today due to the widespread adoption of promises and async/await, but it’s still useful for legacy codebases or specific s. n esting synchronously hrown rrors in avascript /js-testing-asynchronously-thrown-errors , explained that try/catch only works for synchronous logic. or asynchronous callbacks, you need to pass a done argument to the test and call it when the test is complete. he est documentation https://jestjs.io/docs/asynchronous callbacks provides a helpful example of testing asynchronous callbacks. ote that omitting the done callback causes the test to finish before the asynchronous operation completes: javascript // ncorrect: est finishes before callback is invoked test "the data is peanut butter", = { function callback error, data { if error { throw error } expect data .to e "peanut butter" } fetch ata callback } o fix this, we explicitly call done : javascript // orrect: est waits until done is called test "the data is peanut butter", done = { function callback error, data { if error { done error return } try { expect data .to e "peanut butter" done } catch error { done error } } fetch ata callback } e can apply this pattern to test do ask with callbacks: javascript test "task resolves if value < 0.5", code in ava cript requires understanding how promises, async/await , and callbacks work. ach approach has its strengths: - romises: deal for direct promise handling with concise syntax. - sync/await: title: Testing Asynchronously Thrown Errors in Javascript
summary: When testing JavaScript applications, it’s often necessary to verify that expected errors are triggered under specific conditions. However, catching errors in asynchronous logic can be tricky because of how JavaScript handles asynchronous operations.
content: - he ontext the-context - he ause the-cause - he ix the-fix - onclusion conclusion he ontext hen testing ava cript applications, it’s often necessary to verify that expected errors are triggered under specific conditions. owever, catching errors in asynchronous logic can be tricky because of how ava cript handles asynchronous operations. s part of my journey to deepen my understanding of ava cript testing, ’ve been working through the est- riven evelopment for ava cript https://learning.oreilly.com/course/test-driven-development-for/9780137864591/ course. n the section on esting errors from callback functions https://learning.oreilly.com/videos/test-driven-development-for/9780137864591/9780137864591- 1 01 04 02/ , came across this example: javascript function slow peration ith rror a, b, callback { set imeout = { if b === 0 { throw new rror " annot divide by 0" } else { callback a / b } }, 2000 } he accompanying test code looked like this: javascript test "test - incorrect technique", done = { try { slow peration ith rror 10, 0, null } catch err { expect err .to e " annot divide by 0" } done } urprisingly, the test passed. owever, upon closer inspection, it became clear that this was a false positive. he done callback was invoked before the slow peration ith rror function had a chance to finish executing, which meant the target function wasn’t tested at all. urthermore, the code in the catch block would never run, as the error was thrown asynchronously. his led me to investigate why this happens and how to fix it. ere’s what learned. he ause he root cause lies in how ava cript handles asynchronous errors. he try/catch block can only catch errors thrown synchronously . o illustrate, consider the following example: javascript try { throw new rror " his will be caught!" } catch err { console.error " aught:", err.message } console.log " inish running." <br/ bash onsole output aught: his will be caught! inish running. n this case, the error is thrown synchronously, so the catch block captures it and logs the error message. owever, if the error is thrown inside an asynchronous operation, such as a set imeout , the try/catch block will no longer apply because the asynchronous code runs in a different phase of the event loop. ere’s an example: javascript try { set imeout = { throw new rror " his won't be caught!" }, 3000 } catch err { console.error " aught:", err.message } console.log " efore timeout." <br/ bash onsole output efore timeout. ncaught rror: his won't be caught! he try/catch block finishes execution before the set imeout callback is invoked, so the error is unhandled. his behavior explains why the original test code doesn’t work: javascript test "test -
